one_line_takeaway: A critical SoK of 26 ML-based Ethereum smart-contract vulnerability detectors that unifies their divergent vulnerability taxonomies and argues their headline >90% F1-scores are not comparable because of inconsistent, unreleased, noisily-labeled datasets - evidenced by replications that collapse reported reentrancy F1s from ~92%/78% to ~56%/55%.
key_finding: The survey's central RQ4 conclusion is that the field's detectors are effectively incomparable despite most reporting average F1-scores above 90%, because datasets, vulnerability definitions/nomenclature, and labeling methods differ wildly and are rarely released. Its sharpest evidence is a replication discrepancy it draws from Cai et al. [13], who re-evaluated Peculiar [104] and TMP [116] on their own Security-Best-Practices dataset and obtained reentrancy F1 of only 56.45% and 55.41%, versus the 92% and 78.11% originally claimed by those tools. The survey's own tallies find reentrancy is targeted by every one of the 26 papers (RQ1), and that 44.4% of methods build a custom dataset rather than reuse a shared one (RQ3/Fig 3). Root causes flagged: dataset labeling via 2-3 static analyzers by majority vote (inheriting analyzer false positives), biased/error-prone manual labeling, and widespread non-release of code and data.
core_contribution: A systematization-of-knowledge (SoK) / qualitative survey of 26 machine-learning-based vulnerability detectors for Ethereum smart contracts written in Solidity (papers 2018-2024). Its own contributions are: (1) a comprehensive comparative review of the 26 detectors along model, dataset, and vulnerability-coverage dimensions; (2) a unified vulnerability taxonomy (Table 1) that re-maps the inconsistently named vulnerabilities across all surveyed papers into 8 macro-categories aligned to Vidal et al.'s OpenSCV, with CWE/SWC codes, severity, and fix status; (3) answers to four research questions (which vulnerabilities are targeted, which datasets are used, which ML models are used, whether results are comparable); (4) a critical analysis of drawbacks/gaps in the field; and (5) proposed best practices and mitigation strategies. Crucially, almost every performance number in the paper is a surveyed detector's OWN self-reported figure compiled by the authors, not a result the survey itself measured; the survey's genuinely original quantitative outputs are its cross-paper tallies (Figures 2 and 3) and the taxonomy mapping.
threat_model: This is a survey, so it does not introduce a new threat model; instead it catalogs the landscape of Solidity/EVM smart-contract vulnerabilities that the surveyed detectors aim to catch. Adversaries exploit deployed, immutable contracts for financial gain. The paper organizes ~34 vulnerability types into 8 macro-categories: Unsafe External Calls (e.g., Reentrancy - the DAO hack, ~3.6M ETH), Mishandled Events, Gas Depletion, Erroneous Credit Transfer, Bad Programming Practices & Language Weaknesses, Incorrect Control Flow (timestamp/transaction-order dependency), Arithmetic Issues (integer over/underflow), and Improper Access Control (tx.origin auth, unprotected self-destruct). The paper's meta-level 'threat' is methodological: unreliable, non-comparable detector evaluations that give a false sense of security.
limitations: Of the surveyed FIELD (the paper's critique): restricted vulnerability coverage skewed toward reentrancy; dataset construction flaws (small custom sets, unbalanced classes, static-analyzer-derived noisy labels); inconsistent vulnerability nomenclature and label granularity; frequent lack of comparison with related work (especially in security-venue papers); reporting only average F1 rather than per-vulnerability results; heterogeneous metrics; poor replicability (most papers release neither code nor datasets); and usability gaps (missing or high inference time, e.g. >3s or ~10s for some models, and no vulnerability localization). Of the SURVEY itself (self-acknowledged): the taxonomy is not exhaustive (covers only a subset of OpenSCV's vulnerabilities); severity levels in Table 1 are partly inherited from prior work and partly reconstructed from the authors' own experience (subjective); and it inevitably relies on the surveyed papers' self-reported, unverified numbers.

datasets_used: Survey scope: 26 ML-based detectors/papers reviewed (2018-2024), collected from Scopus and Google Scholar; the survey trains nothing itself
Public labeled datasets it catalogs (used by the surveyed tools): CodeSmell (587 real-world contracts, 20 vulnerability types)
SolidiFI (50 contracts injected with 9369 distinct bugs)
Smartbugs Curated (143 annotated contracts, 208 tagged vulnerabilities)
Smartbugs Wild (47518 unique real contracts collected via Etherscan; commonly re-labeled by authors)
ESC (40932 contracts: reentrancy, timestamp dependence, infinite loop)
quantitative_results: SURVEY'S OWN SYNTHESIS - RQ1 (Fig 2): reentrancy (RE) is targeted by all 26 reviewed papers, the single most-considered vulnerability, followed by timestamp dependency (TD) and integer overflow (OF)
SURVEY'S OWN SYNTHESIS - RQ3/dataset choice (Fig 3): custom datasets 44.4%, Smartbugs Wild 19.4%, SolidiFI 8.3%, ESC 8.3%, Smartbugs Curated 5.6%, HuangGai 2.8%, CodeSmell 2.8%, Other 5.6%

SURVEY'S OWN SYNTHESIS - taxonomy (Table 1): ~34 differently-named vulnerabilities re-mapped into 8 macro-categories with CWE/SWC codes and severity (aligned to OpenSCV, which itself uses 7 categories)
SURVEY'S KEY THESIS EVIDENCE (borrowed from Cai et al. [13]): replicated reentrancy F1 of Peculiar [104] = 56.45% and of TMP [116] = 55.41%, versus 92% and 78.11% originally claimed by those tools

COMPILED SELF-REPORTED F1s (Table 3; survey argues these are NOT comparable): Eth2Vec 57.5 (lowest), TMP/DR-GCN [116] 77.13, Li et al. [62] 75.3, DeeSCVHunter [107] 83.4, CNN-BiLSTM [43] 83.63, HGAT [71] 84.25, CGE [68] 85.43, ABCNN [88] 87.66, BLSTM-ATT [79] 88.26, CBGRU [110] 89.93, SmartConDetect [53] 90.9, Cai et al. [13] 90.57, SPCBIG-EC [111] 90.79, Bi-GGNN [12] 91.1, Peculiar [104] 92, Soliaudit [64] 90.4, ESCORT [69] 95, MODNN [112] 94.8, Jain & Tripathi [52] 96.5, ContractWard [100] 97, Song et al. [87] 93, Mi et al. [75] 97, CodeNet [51] 97.63 (highest)
DeeSCVHunter [107] (borrowed): Bi-LSTM+VCS 86.87% F1 on reentrancy; LSTM+attention+VCS 79.93% F1 on timestamp dependency
Inference-time concerns compiled by survey: several detectors exceed 3s/contract; SPCBIG-EC [111] ~9.8s; xFuzz [106] ~30s; CodeNet [51] 0.14s and ESCORT [69] 0.20s among the fastest
Only 2 prior surveys focused on ML for smart-contract vulnerability detection existed ([57] up to 2021, [89]), both critiqued as limited in scope and lacking critical evaluation
baselines_compared: None in an experimental sense - the paper is a survey and runs no benchmarks of its own

It positions itself against the only two prior ML-focused smart-contract vulnerability-detection surveys, [57] and [89], arguing both are limited in width (number of frameworks) and depth (comparative metrics) and offer no critical evaluation of drawbacks or open problems

Note: Fig 1's vulnerability distribution (e.g. Arithmetic 30.4%, RE 11.9%) is sourced from the external Smartbugs-Wild results, NOT computed by this survey; it should not be attributed to the authors' own tally
